coredata.dc:title: Stretchable and self-healable double-network ionogel with strong adhesion and temperature tolerance for information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Zengfeng
Wang, Xiaolin
Wang, Tiantian
Zhao, Xiangjie
Zhang, Jingyue
Xu, Chao
Xu, Jiaheng
Yin, Hongzong
coredata.dc:description: Recently, hydrogels with adjustable mechanical properties have been developed for information security applications. However, the defects such as easy drying and inferior resistance to extreme temperatures limit their extended applications. Herein, a fluorescent double-network ionogel doped with imidazole-modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is constructed by the dynamic covalent cross-linking of chitosan with glutaraldehyde and chemical cross-linking of acrylamide in 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (EMIMCl). The ionogel exhibits high thermal stability and good resistance to high and low temperatures. In addition, the as-prepared ionogel possesses good viscoelasticity with high elastic modulus (G', ∼ 105 Pa) and a viscosity of ∼ 104 Pa·s. Attributed to the excellent stretchability, durability and self-adhesion in a wide operating temperature range, the ionogel has been successfully constructed to a mechanochromic fluorescent device for the reversible encryption and decryption of the information. Dual stimuli of UV light and force are required to reveal the hidden information. Besides, the dynamic Schiff base bonds formed by amine groups of chitosan and aldehyde groups of glutaraldehyde allow the ionogel to quickly repair damages without external stimulation, prolonging its service life. Therefore, this work reports a new strategy for the preparation of soft materials for more secure information encryption by using high-performance ionogels.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic route of imidazole-cation modified CQDs. (b) HR-TEM image of CQDs. Insets are lattice parameter and the size distribution of CQDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of CA, [APMIm][Br] and CQDs. (d) XRD pattern of CQDs. (e) UV–vis (black) and the optimal excitation and emission spectra of the CQD aqueous solution (c CQDs = 0.5 mg·mL−1). (f) Excitation-dependent PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of the CQD aqueous solution (c CQDs = 0.5 mg·mL−1). Insets are photos of the sample under ambient light (left) or UV light (right) of 365 nm, respectively.

(a) Schematic illustration for constructing the CQD-doped DN ionogel. (b) Transmittance of ionogels with a thickness of 1 mm, and the inset is an image of the CQD-doped DN ionogel in front of a logo. (c) Excitation-dependent PL emission spectra of the ionogel with a 7:3 mass ratio of EMIMCl to AM at different excitation wavelengths, and the inset shows the ionogel under 365 nm UV light. (d) SEM image of the ionogel with a 7:3 mass ratio. (e) DSC curves of pure ionic liquid EMIMCl and ionogels with different mass ratios of EMIMCl to AM. (f) TGA curve of the ionogel with a 7:3 mass ratio.

(a) Macroscopic self-healing process of the ionogel with a 7:3 mass ratio of EMIMCl to AM. (b) Microscope images of self-healing process over the time for the scratched ionogel. (c) FT-IR spectra of chitosan, glutaraldehyde and xerogel.

(a) The adhesive and bearing capacity of ionogel at different temperatures of −30, 25 and 100 °C. (b) Adhesive performances of the ionogel to different substrates, including aluminum, glass, copper, plastic, stainless steel, paper, wood and stone.

(a) Photographs of the ionogel with a 7:3 mass ratio bearing different deformations of bending, twisting and stretching under 365 nm UV light. (b) Stretching tests of the ionogel with a 8:2 mass ratio of EMIMCl to AM above liquid nitrogen (∼ −50 °C) and boiling water (∼70 °C).

(a) The variation of G' with the stress for ionogels with different mass ratios. (b) Frequency sweep results for the pure ionogel without CQDs and CQD-doped ionogel. The mass ratio of EMIMCl to AM is 7:3. (c) The variation curves of the viscosity with shear rate for ionogels with different mass ratios. (d) Tensile stress–strain curves for ionogels with different mass ratios of EMIMCl to AM. (e) Cyclic tensile stress-stain curves of the ionogel with a 7:3 mass ratio of EMIMCl to AM. (f) Compressive stress–strain curve for the ionogel with a 7:3 mass ratio of EMIMCl to AM.

(a) Schematic diagram of the double-layer encryption device based on two pieces of ionogels, with dual stimulating responses to UV light and tensile force. (b) Photographs of the device under ambient light (left) and 365 nm UV light (middle and right). The hidden letters or pattern can be observed only when the device is stretched under UV light. The operating temperature is room temperature. (c) Luminescence intensity versus thickness of ionogels with different CQD concentrations relative to AM. The excitation wavelength is 365 nm. (d) Fluorescence spectra for the double-layer devices containing 2 wt% CQDs at a 365 nm excitation wavelength. Red fluorescent ink is painted between two ionogels of the device. (e) Luminescence intensity at 450 nm versus the thickness of one layer of ionogel in the device. (f) Luminescence intensity at 615 nm versus the thickness of one layer of ionogel in the device.

head.ce:title: Stretchable and self-healable double-network ionogel with strong adhesion and temperature tolerance for information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an 271018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taishan University, Tai’an 271000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, hydrogels with adjustable mechanical properties have been developed for information security applications. However, the defects such as easy drying and inferior resistance to extreme temperatures limit their extended applications. Herein, a fluorescent double-network ionogel doped with imidazole-modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is constructed by the dynamic covalent cross-linking of chitosan with glutaraldehyde and chemical cross-linking of acrylamide in 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (EMIMCl). The ionogel exhibits high thermal stability and good resistance to high and low temperatures. In addition, the as-prepared ionogel possesses good viscoelasticity with high elastic modulus (G', ∼ 105 Pa) and a viscosity of ∼ 104 Pa·s. Attributed to the excellent stretchability, durability and self-adhesion in a wide operating temperature range, the ionogel has been successfully constructed to a mechanochromic fluorescent device for the reversible encryption and decryption of the information. Dual stimuli of UV light and force are required to reveal the hidden information. Besides, the dynamic Schiff base bonds formed by amine groups of chitosan and aldehyde groups of glutaraldehyde allow the ionogel to quickly repair damages without external stimulation, prolonging its service life. Therefore, this work reports a new strategy for the preparation of soft materials for more secure information encryption by using high-performance ionogels.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of global informationization, everyone can easily obtain and disseminate information in daily life, leading to great attention to information security [1–4]. Therefore, researchers have developed a variety of technologies and materials for information protection, such as fluorescent ink [5], magnetic ink [6], MOFs [7], plasma tags [8] and gels [9]. Among them, gels have been earnestly studied due to their advantages of diversified designs and tunable properties including mechanical strength and viscoelasticity [10,11]. Tang et al. [1] synthesized a smart fluorescent hydrogel with Fe3+ and H+ responding to fluorescence quenching, and exploited it as an anti-counterfeiting platform for multistage data security protection. Li et al. [4] designed a hydrogel with light-responsive switching behavior, which could be reversibly encrypted and decrypted by simple light irradiation. By using different molar ratios of lanthanide ions, the emission color of the hydrogel could be precisely adjusted and 3D information codes were constructed. These attempts have provided new ideas for the development of information storage and anti-counterfeiting materials based on hydrogels. However, the applications of hydrogels have been restricted by their disadvantages such as narrow applicable temperature range and easy loss of solvents. Therefore, the developments of flexible gel materials with excellent comprehensive performances including appropriate mechanical strength and elasticity, good chemical and thermodynamic stability, wide operating temperature range as well as tunable cryptographic technology are especially critical for information encryption.

Ionogel is a kind of solid-like soft material constructed by the gelation of ionic liquids (ILs) using polymers, low-molecular-weight gelators and inorganic nano materials [12–17]. Combining the advantages of ILs and the matrix, ionogels exhibit many unique properties, such as non-flammability, good chemical stability and high ionic conductivity [18–20]. In addition, ionogels usually possess excellent thermal stability to resist extreme temperatures [21,22] and remarkable endurance to dry conditions. This is because that, IL itself is not easy to volatilize at high temperatures and not easy to freeze at low temperatures when immobilized in the three-dimensional matrix. Therefore, ionogels can effectively overcome the limitations of hydrogels and can be used in exposed environments and under conditions of high and low temperatures. In recent years, the constructions of self-healing ionogels which can intelligently repair damages have extensively explored by introducing noncovalent bonds [19,23,24] and dynamic covalent bonds [25] to the systems. For example, attributed to the synergistic contribution of noncovalent bonds, ionic aggregates and hydrogen bonds, a polysiloxane-supported ionogel designed by Zheng et al. exhibited a healing efficiency of 83% in 12 h at room temperature [19]. D’Anna et al. constructed novel supramolecular ionogels by using didodecylimidazolium-based ([C12C12im]+) salts in different ILs. One ionogel of them exhibited self-healing and self-strengthening ability and could self-healed in 10 min. The good performances made them useful in the application of cationic dye removal [23]. The ability to heal damages effectively make ionogels on the frontline of applications. Due to the significant physical and chemical characteristics, ionogels have been widely used in various fields including lithium batteries [20], supercapacitors [26], sensors [27], actuators [28], and adsorption [29] and so on. However, there is little work concerning applications of ionogels in information encryption. It is highly desirable to design and manufacture new ionogels with tunable properties and satisfactory encryption function to realize easier and safer information protection.

In this work, a chitosan/polyacrylamide (PAM) double-network (DN) ionogel was successfully constructed by simply one-pot in situ photopolymerization of acrylamide (AM) monomers and the cross-linker N, N'-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAA) in the ionic liquid 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (EMIMCl). Besides, a kind of ionic liquid-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is doped into the matrix to endow the ionogel with tunable fluorescence performance. The ionogel achieves comprehensive properties of adjustable mechanical strength and viscoelasticity, good stretchability, excellent adhesion, high thermal stability and wide operating temperature range. In spite of the addition of CQDs, the ionogel has a high transparency of 89%. Here, the as-prepared fluorescent ionogel with adjustable properties is an ideal candidate for information protection, and it is made into a double-layer device with secret information written by commercial fluorescent inks. The light-force synergistic responses make it realize more secure encryption of information. When applying both ultraviolet light irradiation and stretching to the device constructed by double layers of ionogels, we could clearly identify the information that it recorded. The most important thing is that the DN ionogel is stable when operated at high temperature of ∼ 70 °C and low temperature of about −50 °C. The ionogel cannot break easily when subjected to mechanical loads under high and low temperature conditions and recovers in a relatively short time, which effectively broadens the application range of ionogel under severe conditions. In addition, attributed to the dynamic Schiff base, the ionogel can independently repair the internal and external physical damages, and provide higher security, durability and reliability during the information protected process. Therefore, the constructed DN ionogel is expected to be applied as a novel device in the field of information encryption.

Chitosan, 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone (1173), acrylamide, N, N'-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAA, 98%), glutaraldehyde (50 wt% solution in H2O) and citric acid monohydrate (CA, 98%) were purchased from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. (China). Ionic liquids of 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (EMIMCl) and 1-aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([APMIm][Br]) were purchased from Center for Green Chemistry and Catalysis (Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 99%). All chemicals were commercially available and without further purification unless noted otherwise. The resistivity of deionized water used in the experiment was 18.2 MΩ·cm.

Chitosan/PAM DN ionogel was prepared by the one-pot method. Firstly, chitosan (2 wt% relative to AM) and EMIMCl were heated and stirred at 80 °C for 4 h until dissolved. Then AM as the polymerizable monomer, 1173 (1 wt% relative to AM) as photoinitiator, MBAA (1 wt% relative to AM) as cross-linker as well as CQDs (2 wt% relative to AM) were added to the above solution and heated at 80 °C for 10 min by stirring to obtain a homogeneous and clear solution. Unless otherwise noted, the concentrations in the ionogels were adopted as described above. The mass ratios of EMIMCl to AM monomers explored in experiments were 6:4, 7:3 and 8:2. Then the mixed solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene mold and irradiated by UV irradiation at 365 nm for 3 min to obtain the final fluorescent ionogel.

The zeta potential of the dispersions was measured with a laser nanometer particle size analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern, UK). The ionogel was pretreated by solvent exchange method with deionized water to remove ILs, and the cross-section of the xerogel was observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI SU 8010, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) operating on a FT-IR spectrometer (TENSOR II, Bruker, Germany) from 4000 to 400 cm−1 was used to confirm the formation of Schiff base in the gel matrix. The resolution was 4 cm−1 and the scanning rate was 32 cm·s−1. Different states for gradually healed ionogel when damaged were observed by an optical microscope (Axio Scope A1, Precise, China), and the magnification of objective lens is 50X.

An ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV-2450, SHIMADZU, Japan) was used to characterize the transmittance of the ionogel with a thickness of 1 mm from 400 to 800 nm. The fluorescence spectra of samples were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian, USA).

The glass transition temperature of the ionogel was tested from −120 to 50 °C with a heating rate of 10 K·min−1 by the differential scanning calorimeter (DSC 214, NETZSCH, Germany). The thermal decomposition temperature of the ionogel was measured by a thermal weight loss analyzer (DTG-60AH, SHIMADZU, Japan) from 0 to 600 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 under N2 gas atmosphere.

Rheological measurements were performed on a rheometer (MCR 102, Anton Paar, Austria) with a 25 mm diameter parallel plate, and the gap is 1 mm. The oscillatory frequency is fixed to 10 rad·s−1 when carrying out stress sweep measurements. The frequency sweep was performed with the angular frequency ranging from 0.1 to 100 rad·s−1. To obtain the viscosity of ionogels, steady-shear measurements were measured over the shear rate range of 0.001 to 10 s−1. The experiment temperature was controlled at 25 °C.

The mechanical properties of the ionogel were measured by the electronic universal testing machine (WDW-02, China). Tensile test conditions: the sample was dumbbell-shaped, and the stretching rate is 50 mm·min−1. Compression test conditions: the sample was a cylinder with a diameter of 22 mm and a height of 7 mm, and the compression rate is 50 mm·min−1.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the imidazole cation-modified CQDs were successfully prepared from CA and ionic liquid [APMIm][Br] using pyrolysis method according to our reported method [30–33]. Briefly, CA and [APMIm][Br] (molar ratio 1:3) were premixed in water, then the mixture was heated to 180 °C until the water was removed. The following pyrolysis of the mixture was performed at 240 °C for 2 h until a reddish brown dispersion was obtained. Then they were dialyzed with water for 5 days and lyophilized. The structure and properties of CQDs were characterized. High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1b) shows that the prepared CQDs possess uniform particle size and good dispersion. The mean diameter of CQDs was about 6 nm, which was consistent with the reported CQDs which possess quasi-spherical structure and a size less than 10 nm. Well-resolved lattice stripes with 0.23 nm inter-planar spacing can be found in the magnified HR-TEM image (inset of Fig. 1b), which agrees with the lattice plane of graphene (100) face [32,34,35]. As for the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 1c), one can see a variety of peaks at 1694, 1643 and 1566 cm−1 which could be attributed to CO stretching vibration of the carboxyl group, CO stretching vibration of the amide Ι band and the amide II band, respectively [32]. The characteristic NH stretching vibration peak at 3149 cm−1 and CN stretching vibration peak at 1167 cm−1 of [APMIm][Br] remain in the infrared spectrum of CQDs, but the characteristic peak of CA nearly disappears. The above changes in FT-IR spectra indicate that the ionic liquid [APMIm][Br] has been successfully grafted on the periphery of the CQDs, while CA has decomposed completely during the pyrolysis process. In addition, the zeta potential (ζ) of 0.5 mg·mL−1 CQD aqueous solution has been measured to be 28.26 ± 2.8 mV, confirming the positively charge of CQDs contributed by imidazolium moieties. The result of X-ray diffraction (XRD) shows a broad peak at 2θ = 24° (Fig. 1d), which indicates the overall amorphous nature and the amorphous state of the CQDs [30]. We found that the prepared CQD aqueous solution could maintain a long-term homogeneous phase without any obvious precipitation at room temperature. The fluorescence properties of CQDs in both water and ionic liquid EMIMCl were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1e, there is a broad absorption peak in visible region for the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQD aqueous solution. Meanwhile, based on results of excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL), we can draw a conclusion that its optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are at 390 and 460 nm, respectively. The emission wavelength is red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelength (Fig. 1f), which indicates the CQD aqueous solution has a wavelength-dependent excitation behavior [30,36]. The fluorescentproperties of solutions formed by CQDs in EMIMCl were also investigated, as shown in Fig. S1. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for the solution of CQDs in EMIMCl are at 460 and 520 nm, respectively. When the solvent changes from water to EMIMCl, the color emitted by CQD solution under UV light changes from blue to yellowish green (insets in Fig. 1f and Fig. S1b). The emission properties of solutions composed of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) in ILs have been investigated in detail, and the authors found that the fluorescence emission of the solutions increased with the increasing concentration of NCNDs [37]. The relationship between fluorescence intensity of CQD-EMIMCl solution and CQD concentration in present work was investigated (Fig. S1c). The PL intensity first increases and then deceases with the increasing CQD concentration. The downtrend at high CQD concentration is caused by aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [38,39]. The concentration of the solution formed by CQDs in EMIMCl which has maximum PL intensity is around 0.5 mg·mL−1.

Chitosan is a safe, non-toxic, biodegradable and compatible natural polysaccharide, which has good miscibility with ionic liquids [40–42]. According to the previous study, the dissolution of chitosan can effectively regulate the mechanical properties of the ionogel and leads to a fast gelling of ionic liquid [40]. In present work, chitosan as well as AM has been adopted to be the main building blocks to construct a DN ionogel. The formation scheme of the ionogel is shown in Fig. 2 a. A mixture containing chitosan, glutaraldehyde, AM, MBAA, 1173 and CQDs was dissolved in EMIMCl by heating and stirring to form a transparent solution. The hydrogen bonds formed by different components could effectively disrupt the crystal structure of EMIMCl which is solid at room temperature, and thus lead to the formation of homogeneous solutions [41,42]. Then the first network dynamically covalently cross-linked by imine linkages formed via Schiff base reaction between NH2 in chitosan and CHO in glutaraldehyde. Hydrogen bonds between chitosan also play an important role to the first network during the cooling process. Then the polymerization of monomers of AM and the crosslinker of MBAA was triggered by UV irradiation to form the second chemically cross-linking network. Finally, the first chitosan network and the second PAM network collectively form the interpenetrating DN ionogel.

The effects of parameters such as ratios and concentrations of different materials on the performances of ionogels have been explored through a series of experiments, and are discussed detailedly in the section of mechanical property. The parameters have been optimized to get a high-performance ionogel to satisfy the requirements of information protection.

The ionogel shows high transparency due to the good compatibility of different materials. The transparency of the ionogel was characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 2b), where the transmittance value of ionogel without CQDs is determined to be 95% in the visible wavelength range of 400–800 nm. The addition of CQDs reduces the transparency of the ionogel for a certain degree, but it still remains high transparency with a transmittance value of about 89%. We can see the words of “Shigandang of mount Tai” clearly through the ionogel with a thick of 1 mm (inset in Fig. 2b), which further proves the high optical transparency and mechanical stretchability of the ionogel. Fig. 2c shows the fluorescence spectra of the CQD-doped ionogel, and the optimal excitation and emission wavelength is at 380 and 450 nm, respectively. The ionogel exhibits a brown color under natural light, and it emits cyan fluorescence when induced by UV light (inset in Fig. 2c). The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of the CQD-doped ionogel are apparently different from those measured for solutions of CQDs in ionic liquid. The maximum emission peak position experienced a significant shift from 520 nm for the solution of CQDs in ionic liquid (Fig. S1c, excitation wavelength: 460 nm) to ∼ 450 nm for the ionogel (Fig. 2c, excitation wavelength: 380 nm). The wavelength shifts may be explained by the chemical environment change from a solution state to a gel state where there are complicated interactions between CQDs and other components in the three-dimensional network [43]. Though prepared to a quasi-solid gel, the CQDs refrain from aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching for the successful dispersion by the ionogel matrix. The result is different from the reported phenomena of supramolecular ionogels formed by dicationic organic salts [44,45] and nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots [37]. In the report, emission quenching was observed for the gel phases, which was explained to the interactions of carbon nanodots with gelators [37]. To further confirm the microstructure of the ionogel, xerogel was obtained from the ionogel by solvent exchange and freeze drying, and observed by SEM. As exhibited by SEM image in Fig. 2d, the morphology of the ionogel is a typical porous three-dimensional network.

The thermal performances of ionogels have been investigated by DSC and TGA. As shown in the Fig. 2e, DSC curves for both EMIMCl and ionogels with different ratios of EMIMCl to AM have been measured. The DSC curve of EMIMCl reveals a crystallization point at about −89 °C and a glass transition temperature (T g) at around −96 °C for EMIMCl. As for ionogels, it is obviously that there are only glass transition temperatures with lower values at about −90 ∼ −100 °C and it is even negligible for the ionogel with a ratio of 6:4. The only existence of T g indicates the high homogeneity of the ionogel [46]. The low T g values reveal the strong low-temperature resistance of ionogels and they are urgently required for components in the matrix to move freely to guarantee the self-healing process [27]. Due to the good thermal stability and non-volatility properties of ILs, compared to hydrogels, ionogels generally possess extremely strong tolerance to high temperatures which was demonstrated by TGA. It has reported that the thermal decomposition temperature of pure ionic liquid EMIMCl is up to 285 °C [47], and the decomposition temperature of the ionogel is ∼ 250 °C (Fig. 2f), revealing the good thermal stability of the ionogel. The high thermal stability makes the as-prepared ionogel can be applied in a wide range of temperatures.

The DN ionogels possessing self-healing property have a prolonged lifetime because they could restore their original state after healing the external damages, which is highly desirable in practical applications. As shown in Fig. 3 a, macroscopic self-healing experiments were performed on an ionogel to observe its self-healing ability visually. A complete sample was cut in half with a knife, and the two fractured parts could heal in a short time after the close contact without any external intervention or addition of healing agents. The healed ionogel can be stretched without cracking, and the original shape can be restored after releasing the force, which proving the good self-healing property and the fast recovery of mechanical strength for the ionogel. The self-healing ability of the ionogel was further explored by an optical microscope and the results are shown in Fig. 3b. The ionogel started to heal the breakages immediately after the severing, and the incision gradually disappeared in 2 min. We speculate that there are three main reasons for the outstanding self-healing property. First, imine bonds are formed at the interface of the ionogel through Schiff base reaction. Fig. 3c provides a detailed FT-IR spectra for the comparison of chitosan, glutaraldehyde and the xerogel obtained by removing ILs from ionogels. The characteristic bands of chitosan at 1594 and 1660 cm−1 can be assigned to the swing and torsional vibration of NH in amino groups, and the characteristic absorption band of glutaraldehyde at 1708 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the carbonyl groups. As for the FT-IR spectrum of xerogel, we can see that the NH group of chitosan at 1594 and 1660 cm−1 and the CO group of glutaraldehyde at 1708 cm−1 disappear. Meanwhile, for the xerogel, a new unique absorption band corresponding to the CN group is observed at 1654 cm−1. The above changes in FT-IR spectra prove that the imine bonds are formed via Schiff base reaction in the ionogel system. The dynamically reversible Schiff base bonds drive the rapid self-healing of the ionogel during the fracture and healing process [48–50]. Secondly, the regularity of the DN polymer networks is disrupted by the addition of the imidazole-modified CQDs, which leading to the reduce of the crystallinity. Therefore, more PAM chain segments without physical crystallization can move freely, thus speeding up the self-healing process. Thirdly, the interactions between amino groups in PAM and chitosan, hydroxyl groups in chitosan, and the N and O atoms on the surface of CQDs can form a dense three-dimensional hydrogen-bond network, and it also promotes the self-healing efficiency [38]. The above reasons account for the self-healing of the ionogel rather than a simple adhesion.

The prepared ionogel has a high thermal decomposition temperature and a low glass transition temperature (Fig. 2e, f), and it can keep good flexibility in a wide temperature range. Besides, the ionogel shows a strong adhesiveness over a wide temperature range. Fig. 4 a shows the adhesive performance of an ionogel (25 mm × 25 mm × 2 mm) at different temperatures. The ionogel was kept at −30, 25 and 100 °C respectively for 5 h in advance. Then the ionogel was used to stick a glass slide and a weight with 100 g together quickly. Obviously, it can firmly and easily adhere and lift the weight, indicating its good temperature adaptability and excellent adhesion which is not affected by high and low temperatures. To further estimate the adhesive ability of the ionogels, the adhesive duration time of ionogel adhering to the weight at different temperatures was roughly tested. As demonstrated in Fig. S2, the adhesive performances of the ionogels at room temperature and low temperature were nearly the same. The adhesion has lasted for days, and no sign of separation was observed during the test period. The adhesive ability of the ionogels was weakened at high temperature. At 100 °C, the adhesive duration time was measured for about 30 min to 1 h. We speculated that the enhanced interactions between different components in ionogels at high temperature led to the poor adhesion to other substrates. Moreover, the ionogel can be easily adhered to various substrates, such as aluminum, glass, copper, plastic, stainless steel, paper, wood and stone (Fig. 4b). Chain segments in the ionogel can diffuse to the surface of substrates, then various interactions formed by functional groups such as carbonyl, amino and hydroxyl groups endow the ionogel with a strong adhesion performance. Taking the wood and paper for example, hydroxyl groups of the cellulose in them can form dense hydrogen bonds with the amino groups and carbonyl groups of chitosan and PAM in the matrix. In addition, the oxygen atom in the cellulose has an ionic dipole interaction with the imidazole ions in ILs. As for glass, amine groups in the ionogel matrix can form hydrogen bonds with silicon oxygen double bonds on the surface of glass. Meanwhile, oxygen anions on the surface of glass can form ionic bonds with imidazole cations in the ionogel matrix. The high adhesion strength between ionogel and metals such as aluminum, stainless steel and copper is due to the formation of high-strength metal coordination and electrostatic interactions [46,51–53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flexibility of ionogel in a wide temperature range
ce:section.ce:para: Good stretchability and mechanical property are necessary for the application of ionogel as flexible devices. The ionogel in present work can be easily processed into different shapes by using chosen mold. The dumbbell-type ionogel can withstand a variety of deformations including bending, twisting and stretching as shown in Fig. 5 a. More importantly, the ionogel can recover its original shape after withdrawing external forces, indicating its good flexibility and mechanical properties. The interpenetrating double networks in the matrix are crucial to mechanical properties of the ionogel. The porous three-dimensional networks allow the polymer matrix to absorb more ionic liquids, then more energy can be absorbed during deformation. During the deformation process, the hydrogen bond fracture among different components in two networks caused dissipation to the external energy. The dynamically reversible imine bonds can also lead to energy dissipation and the shape recovery [46,54].

The elasticity of gels is inevitably affected by the temperature since all solvents have freezing and boiling points [47]. Hydrogels will lose their elasticity when water freezes below 0 °C, or evaporates at high temperatures, which immensely limiting their applications under harsh conditions. In the present work, an imidazole-type ionic liquid EMIMCl with a low T g value and a high thermal decomposition temperature was chosen to be as the dispersive solvent to construct the ionogel, guaranteeing the good elasticity of ionogels at freezing or high temperatures. As shown in Fig. 5b, the ionogel could withstand a large stretching over the surface of liquid nitrogen (about −50 °C) and boiling water (about 70 °C) and then recovered to the original state in a short time after the tensile force was released. The anti-freezing property and high-temperature tolerance make the ionogel a good candidate for flexible devices which can be operated in a wide temperature range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological behaviors and mechanical properties of ionogel
ce:section.ce:para: The DN construction of the ionogel plays an important role in improving its mechanical properties. Gong et al. have firstly introduced DN structure into gel systems in 2003 [55], and pointed out that it can lead to the formation of high-strength hydrogels by combining different networks with appropriate structures. Since then, researchers have attempted to introduce DN structure to ionogels and successfully prepared a series of DN ionogels with high performances [46,56–59]. The mechanical properties of the ionogel were further investigated by rheological characterizations, tensile and compression tests.

The dynamic viscoelasticity of ionogels which is closely related to the stretching ability of the ionogels during application, was evaluated using rheological tests (Fig. 6 a–c). Generally, the viscoelastic gels simultaneously store and dissipate energy and they can be evaluated by both storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G''). First, stress sweep measurements were performed for ionogels with different mass ratios at 25 °C, and the variation curves of G' with the increasing shear stress (τ) are shown in Fig. 6a. The mechanical strength can be evaluated by the G' value, and it is obvious that all ionogels exhibit good mechanical strength. For example, the 6:4 ionogel possesses a high G' value up to 105 Pa. The mechanical strength increases with the increasing monomers, that is, the second network formed by PAM and cross-linker MBAA contributes to the mechanical strength and elasticity of the ionogel significantly. To investigate the influence of the addition of CQDs on the mechanical strength of ionogels, we performed stress sweep measurements for both CQD-doped ionogel and pure ionogels without CQDs. As shown in Fig. S3, there is nearly no difference in G' values of two samples. The G' value can also be adopted to estimating the degree of resistance against mechanical disturbance. Therefore, the results in Fig. S3 indicate that DN structure of the ionogel played a dominating role in resisting the external force and endowed the ionogels with good mechanical properties. Fig. 6b shows results of frequency sweep measurements for ionogels with and without CQDs, and both G' and G'' increase with the increasing angular frequency. It is notable that the values of G' are higher than those of G'', suggesting the domination of the elasticity for ionogels, which is consistent with the typical characteristic of gel materials [4,13,50,60]. The ionogels possessing appropriate storage modulus G' and loss modulus G'' could effectively resist deformation during application of information encryption. Meanwhile, good mechanical viscoelasticity made the ionogels avoid stiffness and fracture when stretched. Similarly, the doping CQDs have little influence on the mechanical properties of pure ionogel, which further proved the aforementioned conclusion. In a previous report concerning hybrid diimidazolium-based ionogels, D’Anna and Prato et al. have investigated that the dispersing nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots could increase gel strength and the resistance of the gel to flow [37]. We attribute the difference to the different gel matrix. In the supramolecular matrix, CQDs are important components in the matrix for the strong interactions with gelators, and the amount of CQDs has influence on mechanical strength [37]. In present work, DN polymeric structure absorbs energy through deformation and guarantees the mechanical viscoelasticity of ionogels. Fig. 6c shows variations of the viscosity with shear rate for ionogels with different ratios. One can see that all ionogels possess high viscosity, and the high viscosity value is almost up to an order of magnitude of 105 for ionogel with 6:4 mass ratio. The additions of both polysaccharide and ILs can enhance the good viscosity of the ionogel, and the results agree well with the adhesive tests for the ionogel to a variety of substrates.

The mechanical properties of the ionogel are influenced by mass ratios of EMIMCl to AM, chitosan concentrations and MBAA concentrations, and a series of tensile tests were conducted to investigate the effects of these factors. Firstly, the influence of chitosan concentrations was investigated, and the tensile stress–strain curves of ionogels with different chitosan concentrations are shown in Fig. S4a. The optimal concentration of chitosan was determined to be 2 wt% relative to AM. Low concentrations of chitosan below 2 wt% lead to the decreasing fracture elongation. When the concentration was higher than 2 wt%, a solution with high viscosity before UV radiation would be obtained. The unavoidable increasing bubbles which are difficult to eliminate will affect the mechanical properties of the prepared ionogel. Fig. S4b shows the influence of the MBAA concentration to tensile strength and strain of the ionogel. Higher tensile fracture stress and lower tensile fracture strain were obtained for the ionogel with a high MBAA concentration, it is because the increasing chemical cross-linking degree makes the ionogel tougher. Although a better fracture elongation was observed for the ionogel with low MBAA concentration, its low formability and high viscosity limited the application. Taking both rigidity and elongation into consideration, the amount of MBAA was determined to be 1 wt% to AM. Finally, different weight ratios of EMIMCl to AM were investigated. As the ratio increased from 6:4 to 8:2, the tensile fracture strain of the ionogel increased from 190% to 479%, with a decrease in tensile fracture stress from 0.13 to 0.03 MPa (Fig. 6d). When the ionogel was constructed with high IL content, its moldability and operability was lower. Considering the balance between rigidity and elongation of the ionogel, we determined that the optimum weight ratio of EMIMCl to AM was 7:3. To sum up, an ionogel with a mass ratio of 7:3 between EMIMCl and AM, 2 wt% chitosan and 1 wt% MBAA, was selected for other experiments.

Mechanical durability is also important for flexible devices based on ionogels. The fatigue resistance of the chosen ionogel was investigated by cyclic tensile stress–strain experiments during loading and unloading under the re-determined strains. As shown in Fig. 6e, the stretching curve and the recovery curve cannot be completely overlapped during the first cycle, and the hysteresis phenomenon is a typical characteristic of the DN structure [46]. Both the extraction of chitosans from the first network and the chain dissociation of PAM in the second network dissipate the external energy during the stretching process, while the damaged network structure could not fully recover during the unloading process, generating the hysteresis phenomenon. The ionogel shows a similar stress–strain curves in the following loading–unloading cycles, but the hysteresis phenomenon is lower compared to the first cycle, indicating a limited energy dissipation of the structure and a stable DN structure. The non-covalent interactions especially the abundant hydrogen bonds and the dynamic Schiff base in the ionogel are partially disrupted when the ionogel was subjected to a tensile strain, and then could be reformed rapidly to avoid free chain sliding and maintain mechanical properties. As shown in the Fig. 6f and Fig. S5, the compressive stress–strain test illustrates the significant compressive resistance of the DN ionogel. The ionogel could sustain more than 330 kPa at 93% strain.

Many efforts have been made to realize the information protection [61–65]. Usually, the information encryption process can be realized by using conventional luminescent materials, however, they are normally visible under ambient or UV light. Stimulus-responsive luminescent materials that rely on the constant addition of chemicals have also been developed for information protection. To overcome drawbacks of conventional information protected technologies, such as visualization under ambient or UV light and the constant addition of invasive chemicals, scientists should devote to develop advanced materials for information encryption.

Recently, hydrogels have shown great potential in the field of information security due to their advantages of diversified designs and tunable properties [1–2]. In this work, the CQD-doped ionogel is combined with commercial fluorescent ink to construct a double-layer device for information encryption. The construction process is shown in Fig. 7 a, one piece of CQD-doped ionogel is stuck on another one which has been printed information with fluorescent ink, and the combination is easy and tight for the good self-adhesion of the ionogel. The preparation has been proved to be easy operating and cost saving.

The double-layer device combines advantages of the UV-absorption and fluorescence capacity of CQDs and the flexibility and durability of DN ionogel. In natural light, the ionogels are brown and the information written by fluorescent ink is colorless. Under the UV irradiation, the ionogel appears cyan due to the UV light absorbing and fluorescence emission by the CQDs. For that reason, the ionogel can prevent the UV light from entering into the inner, and the information is hidden and protected. The thickness of the ionogel decreases when stretching the device. As a result, it impairs the UV absorption capacity of the ionogel and increases the light transmittance due to the Poisson's effect [38,66], leading to the information disclosure. As shown in Fig. 7b, the encrypted information written by fluorescent ink is invisible under natural light, and still cannot be read under UV light in the absence of ionogel deformation. Once the stretching occurred under UV light, we can clearly observe the hidden blue letters “SDAU” (the abbreviation for Shandong Agricultural University) and the red maple leaf pattern. Therefore, both UV irradiation and stretching are indispensable for the information retrieval. When the stress was released, the inner information was hidden again, realizing a dynamic process for encrypting and decoding the information. The encryption performances at different temperatures of about −50, 25 and 70 °C have also been investigated, as shown in Fig. S6. The hidden information could not be observed without stretching. Once stretching, we could easily realize the decryption of hidden pattern at different temperatures, and it further proved the good mechanical performances of ionogels under harsh conditions. The fluorescence intensity of the ionogel can be adjusted by changing the doping CQD amount. As shown in Fig. 7c, the ionogel containing 2 wt% CQDs relative to AM exhibits the highest fluorescence intensity, and ACQ can also be observed for ionogels with CQD concentrations higher than 2 wt%. The results simultaneously demonstrate that the luminescence intensity of ionogels increases with the increasing ionogel thickness. Taking the double-layer device with encrypted information written by red fluorescent ink as an example, we further investigated the mechanism for the encryption and decryption of the information. As shown in Fig. 7d, the PL spectra of the device show two main peaks. One peak at 450 nm is corresponding to CQDs and the other peak at 615 nm is related to the red ink (Fig. S7). The intensity of the peak at 450 nm gradually decreases with the decreasing thickness of the ionogel, meanwhile, the peak at 615 nm increases significantly (Fig. 7d-f). That is, the red emission of the internal information is enhanced when ionogels thin out with stretching, so the encoded information is revealed. Obviously, the constructed device with light-force synergistic responses provides safer hiding and display in terms of information encryption.

In summary, in this paper, a fluorescent DN ionogel was successfully constructed by incorporating CQDs in an ionic liquid EMIMCl which was immobilized in two networks entangled by chitosan and PAM. The abundant hydrogen bonds between different components, dynamic covalent bonds and chemical crosslinking endow the ionogel with adjustable mechanical performance to meet requirements of the gel application. The comprehensive properties of the ionogel include good flexibility and stretchability, appropriate mechanical strength and viscoelasticity, remarkable adhesion. It was revealed that the ionogel cross-linked by dynamic Schiff base formed by amine groups of chitosan and aldehyde groups of glutaraldehyde, was equipped with excellent self-healing property. It could repair the internal and external physical damages autonomously without any external stimuli, leading to a safer and longer application. Thermodynamic tests show that the ionogels possessed good thermal stability including high decomposition temperatures and low glass transition temperatures. Besides, it could be freely stretched at the high temperature of ∼ 70 °C and the low temperature of about −50 °C repeatedly, and could gain reconversion as force was removed. It demonstrated the good thermo-mechanical stability and frost resistance of the ionogel, therefore, it can be operated in a wide temperature range. A double-layer device based on the ionogels was successfully constructed to realize the information encryption. Although the ionogel exhibited a high optical transparency in the visible light range of 400–800 nm with a transmittance value of 89%, the encoded information protected between two layers of ionogels is invisible under ambient light and UV irradiation. The information would not be disclosed until the device was stretched under UV light. The UV absorption capacity as well as the protection capability for encoded information of the device was weakened when the thickness of the ionogel decreased during the stretching process. Thus, the information could be decrypted under dual external stimulations. The device based on the ionogel is more effective and secure than the traditional information protection technologies with single encryption strategy. Considering the simple preparation, the satisfactory comprehensive performances, excellent self-healing property and outstanding temperature tolerance, the ionogel in the work is an ideal material for information encryption. The study would provide insights for the design and development of information encryption technology based on the ionogel, with features of high stability in the exposed or extreme environments, good flexibility, and wide operating temperature range.
